sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa dell'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche tra Iran e Israele, con un impatto negativo sui mercati azionari e un aumento della domanda di asset rifugio. Il VIX è sceso del 3.57% a 22.67, ma rimane sopra la soglia di 20, indicando un mercato ancora difensivo. Il DXY è sceso dello 0.51% a 99.84, mentre l'EUR/USD è salito dello 0.81% a 1.15. L'S&P 500 è salito dell'1.01% a 6699.38, ma il trend è debole.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to rising geopolitical tensions between Iran and Israel, negatively impacting equity markets and increasing demand for safe-haven assets. The VIX decreased by 3.57% to 22.67, but remains above 20, indicating a defensive market. The DXY decreased by 0.51% to 99.84, while EUR/USD increased by 0.81% to 1.15. The S&P 500 increased by 1.01% to 6699.38, but the trend is weak.

market_impact_summary.it: L'aumento delle tensioni tra Iran e Israele ha spinto il prezzo del petrolio in salita del 2.38% a $102.59 al barile, con un impatto negativo sui mercati azionari. L'S&P 500 ha guadagnato l'1.01%, ma il trend è debole. Il TLT è salito dello 0.77% a 87.21, con un rendimento del 10Y USA in calo dello 0.33% a 4.20%. L'oro è salito dello 0.56% a $5022.20/oz.
market_impact_summary.en: The escalation of tensions between Iran and Israel pushed oil prices up by 2.38% to $102.59 per barrel, negatively impacting equity markets. The S&P 500 gained 1.01%, but the trend is weak. TLT increased by 0.77% to 87.21, with a 10Y US yield decrease of 0.33% to 4.20%. Gold increased by 0.56% to $5022.20/oz.

audio_script_it:
Buongiorno a tutti, iniziamo il nostro aggiornamento quotidiano sui mercati. 

Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa dell'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche tra Iran e Israele, con un impatto negativo sui mercati azionari e un aumento della domanda di asset rifugio. Questa situazione ha portato a una riduzione dell'appetito per il rischio, come evidenziato dall'indice Vix, che, sebbene sia sceso del 3.57% a 22.67, rimane sopra la soglia di 20, indicando un mercato ancora difensivo.

Passando ai dati di mercato, l'S&P 500 ha guadagnato l'1.01% a 6699.38, ma il trend è debole. Il cambio euro dollaro è salito dello 0.81% a 1.15, mentre l'indice del dollaro è sceso dello 0.51% a 99.84. Il prezzo del greggio Brent ha guadagnato il 2.38% raggiungendo quota 102.59 dollari al barile. L'oro ha guadagnato lo 0.56% a 5022.20 dollari l'oncia. Bitcoin si attesta intorno ai settantamila dollari, con una perdita dell'1.54%.

Sul fronte geopolitico, la situazione tra Iran e Israele continua a essere fonte di preoccupazione. La Federal Reserve, la BCE e la Banca d'Inghilterra dovranno esprimersi questa settimana sul potenziale impatto del conflitto sull'inflazione. La Cina ha visto la sua economia riprendersi prima dell'escalation della crisi iraniana, ma avverte dei rischi geopolitici. La banca centrale australiana ha alzato i tassi d'interesse per affrontare l'incertezza.

La Banca Centrale Europea ha mantenuto invariati i tassi d'interesse, ma il suo presidente ha sottolineato la necessità di monitorare l'evoluzione del conflitto in Medio Oriente. Il PIL dell'Eurozona è cresciuto dello 0.2% nel quarto trimestre del 2025. I dati macroeconomici statunitensi più recenti mostrano un tasso di inflazione del 2.7% e un tasso di disoccupazione del 4.4%.

Questa settimana saranno pubblicati importanti dati macroeconomici, tra cui il PCE Core YoY e il GDP QoQ USA. La Fed e la BCE dovranno tenere conto di questi dati nella loro prossima decisione sui tassi d'interesse.

In conclusione, il mercato rimane in attesa delle decisioni delle banche centrali e dei dati macroeconomici che saranno pubblicati questa settimana. La situazione geopolitica in Medio Oriente continua a essere fonte di incertezza e potrebbe avere un impatto significativo sui mercati finanziari.

Per quanto riguarda i dati specifici da monitorare, segnaliamo che il PCE Core YoY sarà pubblicato il 28 marzo, mentre il GDP QoQ USA sarà pubblicato il 29 aprile. La prossima decisione sui tassi d'interesse della Fed è prevista per la fine di marzo, mentre la BCE si riunirà ad aprile.

In questo contesto, gli investitori dovranno essere pronti a reagire alle notizie provenienti dal Medio Oriente e ai dati macroeconomici che saranno pubblicati nei prossimi giorni.
audio_script_en:
Welcome to your daily morning market briefing.

Market sentiment is risk-off with a score of 6/10 due to rising geopolitical tensions between Iran and Israel. This negatively impacts equity markets and increases demand for safe-haven assets. The VIX decreased by 3.57% to 22.67 but remains above 20, indicating a defensive market. The dollar index decreased by 0.51% to 99.84, while the euro-dollar exchange rate increased by 0.81% to 1.15. The Standard and Poor's 500 increased by 1.01% to 6699.38, but the trend is weak.

Let's analyze the markets asset by asset. The S&P 500 index rose slightly, but its trend remains weak as investors seek safer assets amid geopolitical uncertainty. The Vix index, which measures market volatility, decreased but still remains above 20, indicating that investors are cautious. The dollar index dropped slightly, reflecting a minor shift towards riskier assets, but this movement is limited. The euro-dollar exchange rate increased, suggesting a slight preference for the euro over the dollar.

The Treasury bond ETF increased by 0.77% to 87.21, indicating a flight to quality. The US 10-year yield decreased by 0.33% to 4.20%, reflecting a decrease in investor appetite for riskier assets. Gold prices increased by 0.56% to $5022.20 per ounce, as investors seek safe-haven assets. The Brent crude oil price rose by 2.38% to $102.59 per barrel, driven by concerns over supply disruptions due to the Iran-Israel tensions.

The Stoxx six hundred European index increased by 0.69% to 59.65, while the Nikkei dropped by 0.09% to 53,700, reflecting mixed sentiment in Asia. The Shanghai index decreased by 0.85% to 4049.91, possibly due to concerns over China's economic rebound and its impact on global markets.

Geopolitics play a significant role in today's market movements. The escalating tensions between Iran and Israel have raised concerns over a broader conflict in the Middle East. This situation could lead to supply chain disruptions, particularly in the energy sector, and impact global economic growth. The international community is closely monitoring the situation, and any significant developments could have immediate effects on financial markets.

On the macroeconomic front, the Federal Reserve's current stance is being closely watched. The last available data on the Fed funds rate, from February, showed it remained at 3.64%. The next PCE Core YoY data release is scheduled for March 28, which could provide insights into inflation trends and influence the Fed's future policy decisions.

In the Eurozone, the European Central Bank's recent decision to keep interest rates at 2.00% is seen as a cautious move to balance economic growth and inflation. The next CPI YoY Eurozone data release is scheduled for April 2, which will be crucial in determining the ECB's future actions.

Looking ahead, investors will be closely watching several key data releases and events. The PCE Core YoY data release on March 28 will provide insights into US inflation trends. The GDP QoQ USA data release on April 29 will offer a comprehensive view of the US economy's performance. Additionally, the CPI YoY Eurozone data release on April 2 and the Disoccupazione EU data release on April 1 will be crucial in determining the ECB's future policy actions.

In conclusion, the market's risk-off sentiment is driven by geopolitical tensions between Iran and Israel. Investors are seeking safe-haven assets, and the situation in the Middle East is being closely monitored. Key data releases and events in the coming days will provide insights into the global economy's performance and influence market movements.

articles.title: How Does the Iran War Affect China’s Energy Security?
articles.snippet: Since the beginning of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, a key question constantly asked is how Beijing might react — assuming that China’s economy relies on oil imported from Iran and shipped through the Strait of Hormuz.In reality, however, China is 85 percent energy self-sufficient. While China imports more than 10 percent of its global oil total from Iran, its energy supply has long been diversified internationally and electrified domestically to avoid critical dependence on any single source. Beijing has built a cushion against a short-term supply shock from a war in the Middle East. articles.title: Conflict and Consequences: The Global Impact of the New Middle East War
articles.snippet: The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran is entering its third week, with no end yet in sight. Crisis Group experts offer a 360-degree view of its effects on peace, security and economic stability around the world.

articles.title: Finding an Off-ramp in the Middle East War
articles.snippet: While the U.S. and Israel have damaged Iran in the Middle East war, Tehran has expanded the conflict. With neither side positioned for a decisive win, the escalation risks are sobering. Diplomacy is crucial to prevent renewed hostilities, but an immediate ceasefire is the priority.

articles.title: Escalation or off-Ramp in the Gulf? And What Hope for a Ceasefire in Lebanon?
articles.snippet: This week on Hold Your Fire!, Richard speaks with Crisis Group’s former President Robert Malley about the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, whether U.S. President Donald Trump will escalate or seek an off-ramp and what Iran might do. He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

articles.title: Iraq Negotiates with Iran to Reopen Vital Oil Shipping Route
articles.snippet: The federal Iraqi government is in contact with Iran to persuade Tehran to allow some Iraqi oil tankers to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Iraq’s Oil Minister Hayyan Abdul Ghani said on Tuesday. “There is communication with Iran regarding allowing the passage of some Iraqi oil tankers,” the minister said in statements carried by the Iraqi News Agency (INA). articles.title: China Built a Massive Oil Buffer Ahead of the Iran Crisis
articles.snippet: China expanded its crude oil stockpiles over the first two months of the year, thanks to higher imports and a ramp-up in domestic production, Reuters’ Clyde Russell reported, adding that the stock buildout strengthened despite higher refining rates in the period. Imports of crude oil to China averaged 11.99 million barrels daily, Russell said, adding that domestic production over the period had averaged 4.42 million barrels daily, for a total supply of some 16.41 million barrels daily. Refineries, however, processed an average of 15.17 million…

articles.title: What Happens If Gulf Producers Deploy ‘Nuclear Option’ To End Middle East War?
articles.snippet: The shutdown of commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has suddenly handed Gulf oil producers enormous leverage over the escalating Middle East war. With roughly 15 million barrels per day of crude exports effectively stranded, Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) nations could deploy what amounts to an energy “nuclear option”: declaring force majeure across their oil and gas exports and deliberately removing another 20% of global supply from the market. Such a move, unpacked in an opinion piece in Middle East Eye, would instantly…

articles.snippet: For years, Iran -- together with its long-term sponsors China and Russia -- has been increasing pressure on neighbouring Iraq to remove the remaining powers from its semi-autonomous northern region and subsume it as a regular governorate into a single unified country, firmly rooted in Beijing and Moscow’s sphere of influence. articles.title: The Invisible Metals Powering a Trillion-Dollar Economy
articles.snippet: American innovator REalloys (NASDAQ: ALOY) is bringing rare earth metals, the backbone of the U.S. defense industry and the power behind a massive civilian economy at China’s mercy, back to North American soil at a critical time for the world.Electric vehicles, consumer electronics, industrial robotics, and artificial intelligence infrastructure all critically rely on permanent magnets manufactured from rare earth alloys that North America hasn’t been able to produce for decades.
